title: Warm compresses
summary: Warm compresses are a comfort measure for facial pressure, ear discomfort or muscle aches. They have little direct evidence for altering cold or flu biology.
evidenceSnapshot: Traditional use and plausible sensory relief; no strong evidence for faster recovery.

interventions.name: Warm face compress
interventions.finding: May reduce the perception of sinus pressure and relax facial muscles.
interventions.practical: Apply comfortably warm cloth for 10–15 minutes.
interventions.mechanism: Heat and touch modulate pain sensation.
interventions.cautions: Avoid very hot packs and prolonged pressure.

interventions.name: Warm neck or chest compress
interventions.finding: May ease muscle soreness from coughing.
interventions.practical: Use a wrapped heat pack on intact skin.
interventions.cautions: Avoid sleeping on electric heat packs.

interventions.name: Warm ear compress
interventions.practical: Hold warm cloth against the outer ear.
interventions.cautions: Does not treat middle-ear infection; no drops or substances into the ear without advice.

practicalSteps: Test temperature on your inner wrist first.
Use brief sessions and check skin afterward.
Combine with evidence-based pain relief if appropriate.
avoidOrCare: Reduced sensation, diabetes, poor circulation and very young children increase burn risk.
Do not delay assessment for severe ear or eye symptoms.

myths.reality: External heat may soothe pain but does not extract viruses or bacteria.

myths.claim: A hot compress opens the sinuses permanently.
myths.reality: Any improvement is temporary comfort, not a structural or antimicrobial effect.

redFlags: Swelling behind the ear, ear discharge or severe worsening pain
unsettled: There are few high-quality trials specifically testing warm compresses for acute respiratory infections.
